question_title: Will the community prediction be higher than 63.00% on 2026-05-14 for the Metaculus question "Will Keir Starmer cease to be Prime Minister of the UK during 2026?"?
summary: This meta-prediction asks whether the Metaculus CP on "Will Keir Starmer cease to be PM during 2026?" will be strictly above 63% on May 14. The CP is currently at exactly 63.00% (May 2), so the question reduces to whether CP will move upward over the next 12 days. The critical event is the UK local elections on May 7, where Labour is expected to suffer catastrophic losses. Active leadership challenge positioning (Streeting with 81+ MPs, Burnham-Rayner coordination) and strong betting market signals (65-80% for 2026 exit) create upward pressure on CP. However, the CP already spiked to 65% in late April and corrected back to 63%, suggesting partial pricing of election expectations. The "sold on the news" effect and Starmer's demonstrated defiance provide downward counterweight. Monte Carlo simulation gives 60-70%, conditional decomposition gives 55-63%, and factor analysis implies ~62.5%. I center at 60%, reflecting moderate confidence that the concrete election catalyst will push CP above 63% but acknowledging meaningful probability of a muted response if results match expectations already incorporated.

factors.description: Local elections May 7 expected to be catastrophic for Labour (losing up to 3/4 of ~2500 council seats, plus Scottish/Welsh elections), providing concrete new negative information that should push CP upward from 63%

Active leadership challenge positioning - Wes Streeting reportedly has 81+ MPs (formal challenge threshold), Burnham-Rayner coordination reported by BBC. A formal challenge before May 14 would spike CP well above 63%

Betting markets strongly favor Starmer leaving in 2026: William Hill 2/7 (~78%), Star Sports 1/4 (~80%), Polymarket Dec 2026 exit at 65.5%. Underlying event probability is high and should be reflected in CP

CP already partially priced in election fears - was at 65% on April 25, declined to 63% by May 2. This 2pp correction suggests forecasters have already incorporated some election expectations, and 'sell the news' effect is possible
Status quo structural bias: CP at exactly 63.00% means strict inequality requires upward movement. Any flat or downward CP = NO. This creates asymmetry favoring NO outcome
Starmer's defiance and Labour 'fightback' narrative. Survived February 2026 'coup that never was' (Al Jazeera). Could stabilize CP if no formal challenge materializes post-elections

reasoning:
## Forecast Summary

**Question**: Will the Metaculus CP on "Will Keir Starmer cease to be PM during 2026?" be above 63% on May 14, 2026?

**Probability: 0.60 (60%)**

### Key Reasoning

**The setup**: CP is at exactly 63.00% (the threshold), so this resolves YES only if CP moves strictly upward. The critical catalyst is the **UK local elections on May 7** (results May 7-8), falling squarely between now and the May 14 resolution date.

**Upward pressure** (~60% weight):
- Labour expected to suffer catastrophic losses (up to 3/4 of ~2,500 council seats)
- Wes Streeting reportedly has 81+ MPs backing a leadership challenge (the formal threshold)
- Betting markets price Starmer's 2026 exit at 65-80%
- Concrete election results will provide new information even if expectations are partially priced in

**Downward/stabilizing pressure** (~40% weight):
- CP already spiked to 65% in late April and corrected to 63% — suggesting partial pricing of election fears
- Starmer survived the February 2026 "coup that never was" and remains defiant
- Status quo (exactly 63%) = NO; structural asymmetry at threshold
- "Sell the news" effect possible if results merely match expectations

**Quantitative validation**: Monte Carlo simulation (60-70%), conditional decomposition (55-63%), factor-implied probability (62.5%) all converge around 60%.
sources_consulted: [Keir Starmer Resignation Odds: Prime Minister Backed To Leave Position In 2026](https://news.williamhill.com/politics/keir-starmer-odds-to-resign-2026/)
resolution_criteria: This question will resolve based on the community prediction of the Metaculus question found [here](https://www.metaculus.com/questions/40967) (i.e. the 'target question'). If the community prediction of the target question on 2026-05-14 19:36:14 is higher than 63.00%, this question will resolve to 'Yes'. If it is lower or equal to 63.00%, this question will resolve to 'No'. If the target question has already resolved before this question opens, then this question will be annulled. If the target question resolves after this question opens, but before 2026-05-14 19:36:14, then this question will resolve to the same value as the target question.

Betting markets strongly favor Starmer leaving in 2026 (~65-80% implied probability), suggesting underlying event probability is high and should be reflected in CP

input.anchor: Meta-prediction base rate: CP at threshold, roughly 50% chance of moving in either direction from current level without strong directional catalyst
input.assessment:
This is a meta-prediction about whether the Metaculus CP on "Will Keir Starmer cease to be PM during 2026?" will be above 63% on May 14. The CP is currently at exactly 63.00% (May 2), meaning status quo = NO (strict inequality).

The critical event is the UK local elections on May 7 (results May 7-8), which fall between now and the resolution date. Labour is widely expected to suffer catastrophic losses. The question is whether this information is already "priced in" to the 63% CP, or whether actual results will drive further upward movement.

Arguments for YES (CP rises above 63%):
- Elections provide CONCRETE new data, even if expectations are partially priced in
- The magnitude and specific patterns of losses matter beyond polls
- Post-election period is when formal leadership challenges typically crystallize
- Multiple challengers are actively positioned (Streeting with 81+ MPs, Burnham, Rayner)
- If a formal challenge is launched, CP would spike well above 63% (likely to 70-85%)
- The underlying probability IS high (betting markets ~65-80% for 2026 exit)

Arguments for NO (CP stays at or below 63%):
- CP DECLINED from 65% to 63% in the week before elections, suggesting partial correction
- Bad elections are expected and may be "priced in" at 63%
- Starmer has survived previous "coup" attempts (February 2026 "coup that never was")
- Without a FORMAL challenge, CP may stay at elevated but stable level
- The threshold is at current value - any flat/downward movement = NO

My Monte Carlo simulation gives ~60-70% depending on assumptions about how much is priced in. My conditional decomposition gives ~55-63%. I'm centering at 60%, which feels appropriate given the asymmetry of a concrete information event (elections) but also the evidence that expectations are partially incorporated.
input.calibration_notes: Anchor is 50% (CP at threshold, random walk). Factors collectively suggest moderate upward pressure (~+0.5 to +0.8 logits), consistent with 60-65%. I'm at 60%, slightly below the Monte Carlo midpoint but above the conditional decomposition midpoint, which feels right given the "already priced in" concern.
input.key_uncertainties:
1. How much of the expected election losses are already incorporated into the 63% CP? The fact that CP was 65% last week and came down suggests partial pricing.
2. Will a formal leadership challenge be triggered before May 14? This is the highest-impact binary uncertainty.
3. How will Metaculus forecasters respond to actual results vs. expectations? New forecasters entering after election results could shift the weighted median.
input.tool_audit:
- research(): Two queries returned useful context on Starmer's current situation and election expectations. First query had rich BBC/Guardian sources. Second query had limited tool access but provided useful baseline data.
- web_search(): Useful for finding prediction market prices and betting odds. Found William Hill, Polymarket, and general betting market data.
- fetch_url(): Polymarket returned empty data (API issue?). William Hill article provided useful specific odds.
- get_cp_history(): Returned 403 (expected for AIB tournament question). Used weekly snapshots from question description instead.
- get_coherence_links(): No related questions found.
- execute_code(): Monte Carlo simulation and conditional decomposition both ran successfully.
input.update_triggers:
1. Formal leadership challenge announced → CP likely spikes to 70%+ (YES)
2. Starmer resigns → underlying question resolves YES (meta-question YES)
3. Elections significantly better than expected → CP drops below 60% (NO)
4. Election results match expectations perfectly → CP stays near 63%, direction uncertain
input.process_reflection: The meta-prediction format is inherently challenging because the CP data for the target question is behind the AIB wall (403). I had to rely on the weekly snapshots provided in the question description, which give a coarse view of CP dynamics. Having daily or hourly CP data would significantly improve forecasting precision. The research tool worked well for gathering UK political context. The conditional decomposition approach felt more grounded than the Monte Carlo because it forced me to think about event-driven channels rather than just CP distributions.

revision_history.reviewer_assessment:
**Verification Summary:**

All six factors are grounded in the research trace:

1. **Factor 1 (catastrophic elections):** BBC sources (Kuenssberg, Chris Mason), Wikipedia all confirm Labour defending ~2,500 seats with potential 3/4 losses. ✅
2. **Factor 2 (Streeting 81+ MPs):** Hounslow Herald and GBNews snippets in trace confirm "more than 81 Labour MPs." Burnham-Rayner coordination confirmed via BBC Kuenssberg article. ✅
3. **Factor 3 (betting markets ~65-80%):** William Hill 2/7 odds (~78%), Star Sports 1/4 (~80%), Polymarket "out by Dec 2026" at 65.5% all confirmed in trace. ✅
4. **Factor 4 (CP 65%→63%):** Weekly CP snapshots in trace show April 25 = 65.00%, May 2 = 63.00%. ✅
5. **Factor 5 (status quo bias):** Resolution criteria correctly state strict inequality (>63.00%). ✅
6. **Factor 6 (Starmer defiance):** Al Jazeera "coup that never was" and Kuenssberg "won every fight" quote both in trace. ✅

**No hallucinated evidence found.**

**Double-counting:** Mild overlap between Factor 1 (elections), Factor 2 (leadership challenge), and Factor 3 (betting markets) — all reflect the same underlying political crisis — but they use distinct evidence channels (news reports, MP counts, market prices). Not severe enough to warrant a flag given the moderate logit values.

**Factor directions:** All correct. Positive logits for upward CP pressure (elections, challenges, markets), negative for downward (priced-in correction, status quo bias, Starmer defiance).

**Resolution criteria engagement:** Agent properly identifies strict inequality, CP at threshold, and the conditional resolution clause. ✅

**Counterargument:** Well-constructed "sold on the news" narrative. Not a token gesture. ✅

**Self-confidence:** 0.40 is appropriate given coarse CP data, 403 on actual CP, and meta-prediction uncertainty. ✅

**Probability check:** Agent's 60% is consistent with evidence. Monte Carlo gives 60-70%, conditional decomposition gives ~58.5%, factor-implied is ~62.5%. The 60% estimate reasonably weights the "already priced in" concern against the concrete election catalyst. My own independent estimate would be 58-62%, so 60% is squarely within range. No meaningful divergence.

**Anchor divergence:** 50% → 60% is a modest 10pp departure well-justified by the dominant election catalyst factor (effective logit +0.75). ✅

**Tool audit minor note:** The audit mentions "research(): Two queries" but metrics show 1 research call (which batched 2 queries internally) — technically accurate. The audit omits mention of kalshi_price (1 call) and polymarket_price (2 calls) that appear in metrics — these may have been auto-invoked by the search infrastructure. Neither omission affects the forecast.

**No worldview contradictions found.** No prior Starmer-related forecasts exist for consistency checking.
